This defense-in-depth approach means that even if the LLM somehow extracts an unauthorized metric name, the warehouse-level security blocks the query from returning data.

q: How would you migrate a 200-metric Looker LookML catalog to the dbt Semantic Layer with minimal analyst disruption?
a: Phase the migration over 6 months in three stages. Stage 1 (months 1-2): audit all LookML explores and measures, identify the top 50 metrics by query frequency using Looker's System Activity model, and build equivalent dbt semantic_models and metrics for those 50. Run Looker and dbt Semantic Layer in parallel, comparing outputs daily via a reconciliation dbt model that subtracts one from the other — any delta above 1% requires investigation before proceeding. Stage 2 (months 3-4): install the dbt-Looker connector to serve the top 50 metrics via Looker explores backed by the dbt Semantic Layer, deprecating the equivalent LookML measures. Migrate the next 100 metrics. Stage 3 (months 5-6): migrate the remaining 50 metrics, complete the Looker connector cutover, and archive the old LookML repository. Throughout, maintain a migration tracker Google Sheet visible to all analysts showing which metrics are in Looker-only, in-parallel, or dbt-only state.
fj: The wrong assumption is that LookML measures can be automatically converted to dbt semantic_model metrics by a code generation script with minimal manual review.
fs: LookML and MetricFlow have fundamentally different semantic models (LookML's type: sum_distinct, fanout-safe derived tables, Liquid templating have no direct equivalent in MetricFlow YAML), so migration requires a human analyst to re-express each metric's business intent in MetricFlow's construct, not just a syntactic conversion.

followups.q: What is the biggest technical gap between LookML's capabilities and MetricFlow's, and how do you bridge it?
followups.a: LookML's type: sum_distinct (which uses Looker's proprietary symmetric aggregates to safely sum a measure across a many-to-one join without fanout) has no direct equivalent in MetricFlow. MetricFlow handles this by refusing the join at query time rather than performing a sum_distinct. The bridge is to pre-aggregate the measure at the correct grain in a dbt model before defining the semantic_model, eliminating the need for a sum_distinct entirely. This requires understanding why the LookML author used sum_distinct (usually because the explore joined to a one-to-many dimension) and redesigning the mart model to remove that ambiguity.

followups.q: How do you handle Looker-specific features like parameter-driven dynamic SQL during migration?
followups.a: Looker's parameters (e.g., a user-inputted currency toggle that changes a SQL expression) have no equivalent in MetricFlow's static YAML. For each LookML parameter, evaluate: (1) if the parameter drives a filter, express it as a fixed set of filtered metric variants in dbt YAML (e.g., revenue_usd, revenue_eur); (2) if the parameter drives a calculation change, pre-compute both variants as separate mart models and semantic_models; (3) if the parameterization is truly dynamic and cannot be pre-computed, keep that specific explore in Looker and do not migrate it to the dbt Semantic Layer. Not every LookML feature should be migrated — accept that the dbt Semantic Layer will handle 80% of queries and Looker retains the power-user edge cases.

followups.q: How do you measure migration success quantitatively?
followups.a: Define a migration scorecard with three key metrics: (1) Metric parity rate — percentage of the 200 migrated metrics whose dbt Semantic Layer output matches Looker output within 0.1% for the trailing 90 days; (2) Query volume shift — percentage of Looker queries for migrated metrics now served via the dbt Semantic Layer connector vs the old LookML explore (tracked in Looker System Activity); (3) Analyst satisfaction score — weekly pulse survey asking analysts to rate 'confidence in metric accuracy' on a 1-5 scale. Target: 95% parity, 80% query volume shift, and satisfaction above 4.0 before declaring the migration complete.

q: How do you use the dbt Semantic Layer in a real-time operational analytics use case where freshness requirements are under 5 minutes?
a: The dbt Semantic Layer in its standard form is batch-oriented — it queries Snowflake tables that are updated by dbt runs, typically on hourly or daily schedules. For sub-5-minute freshness, the architecture requires: (1) a streaming ingestion layer (Snowpipe Streaming, Kafka Connector for Snowflake, or Fivetran Hightouch Streaming) that continuously appends to the Snowflake staging table; (2) an incremental dbt model on the staging table that can be triggered by a Snowflake Task or dbt Cloud job on a 5-minute schedule; (3) the semantic_model wrapping this incremental model. The Semantic Layer API then queries near-real-time data on every call. The operational cost is high: a Snowflake Medium warehouse on a 5-minute schedule with auto-resume runs approximately 288 incremental jobs per day, each consuming warehouse compute. For truly sub-minute freshness, the dbt Semantic Layer is the wrong tool — use Materialize, Apache Pinot, or ClickHouse with a purpose-built OLAP semantic layer instead.
fj: The wrong assumption is that the dbt Semantic Layer can serve real-time metrics natively because MetricFlow queries the warehouse directly at request time.
fs: MetricFlow queries the warehouse at request time but the underlying tables are only as fresh as the last dbt model run; for sub-5-minute freshness you need a streaming ingestion pipeline and a very frequent incremental dbt run schedule, not just the semantic layer API.

followups.q: How does Snowflake Dynamic Tables change the freshness calculus for dbt Semantic Layer?
followups.a: Snowflake Dynamic Tables (GA in 2024) automatically refresh materialized query results when upstream data changes, with a target lag configurable down to 1 minute. By pointing your dbt mart model at a Snowflake Dynamic Table (using dbt's dynamic_table materialization in dbt-snowflake 1.6+), the mart model is always near-real-time without running a dbt job on a schedule. The semantic_model wrapping the dynamic table then serves metrics that are 1-minute fresh on every query. This significantly simplifies the operational architecture for near-real-time semantic layer use cases on Snowflake.

followups.q: What monitoring should you add to detect when metric freshness has degraded in a near-real-time setup?
followups.a: Add a freshness check query to your dbt Cloud job monitoring: SELECT DATEDIFF('minute', MAX(event_at), CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) AS lag_minutes FROM fct_orders_incremental. Alert via Slack or PagerDuty if lag_minutes exceeds your SLA threshold (e.g., 10 minutes). Also monitor the Snowflake Task history for the incremental job to detect failures. In dbt Cloud, set up a source freshness check on the raw source tables so that if the upstream Kafka connector stops loading data, dbt raises a source freshness warning before the metric data goes stale.

followups.q: What are the trade-offs of using a 5-minute dbt incremental job schedule versus a Snowflake Dynamic Table for near-real-time metrics?
followups.a: A 5-minute dbt incremental job gives you full dbt functionality — tests, macros, documentation, and lineage tracking — but adds operational overhead (job monitoring, cost for 288 daily runs) and has a minimum freshness floor of ~5 minutes plus job run time. A Snowflake Dynamic Table achieves sub-minute lag with zero job scheduling overhead, but bypasses dbt's test and documentation framework — you lose the dbt lineage graph node for the mart model and must apply tests separately. The hybrid approach is to use a Dynamic Table for the staging-to-mart transformation and reference it from dbt as an external model (using dbt's sources or external model feature), regaining lineage documentation while benefiting from Dynamic Table freshness.
